text.title: DI Container, Error Hierarchy, and Error Handler Middleware
text.summary: Defines container.ts wiring rules, the AppError hierarchy, and the Express error handler middleware registration order.

rule.statement: PrismaClient must be instantiated at module level in container.ts as a singleton, never inside a function. A PrismaClient instantiated inside a function creates a new connection pool per request, exhausting database connections.
rule.rationale: Prisma connection pools are expensive to create. A module-level singleton shares one pool across all requests. A function-scoped client creates and abandons a pool per request, causing connection exhaustion under load.

rule.statement: container.ts is the single source of truth for all DI wiring: database client → repositories → services → controllers. Feature agents must read container.ts before adding constructor injections to avoid duplicate instantiation.
rule.rationale: Agents regenerating container.ts from scratch tend to duplicate or omit existing entries. Read-before-write prevents overwriting established wiring.

rule.statement: Define the error hierarchy in utils/errors.ts as the first file in any new project: AppError (base), NotFoundError (404), ConflictError (409), UnauthorizedError (401), ValidationError (400). All subsequent code imports from this file.
rule.rationale: All feature code depends on domain errors. Creating errors.ts first provides a stable import target. Features written before errors.ts invent ad-hoc error handling that diverges across files.

rule.statement: The Express error handler middleware must be registered last in app.ts, after all routes. It requires exactly 4 parameters (err, req, res, _next) — a 3-parameter version is not recognized as an error handler by Express.
rule.rationale: Express distinguishes error handlers from regular middleware solely by parameter count. A 3-parameter function is treated as a regular middleware that never receives errors. Registering it before routes means routes never register at all.

rule.statement: The error handler must handle three cases: ZodError (400 Validation), AppError with isOperational=true (use statusCode and code from the error), and all other errors (500 Internal Server Error). Log all errors via the logger before responding.
rule.rationale: Operational errors are expected domain failures. Non-operational errors are bugs. The distinction drives different monitoring and alerting behavior. Both require logging before the response to ensure the log captures the full stack trace.
